name: firestore
context: NoSQL document database with real-time sync, offline persistence, automatic scaling, and declarative security rules for web and mobile apps
info: Cloud Firestore organizes data into collections of documents, where each document contains fields (strings, numbers, booleans, timestamps, arrays, maps, references, geopoints) and can hold subcollections for hierarchical data. CRUD operations use the v9 modular SDK: addDoc/setDoc to create, getDoc/getDocs to read, updateDoc to modify, and deleteDoc to remove. Queries support where clauses with comparison and array operators, orderBy, limit, startAfter for cursor-based pagination, and compound queries backed by composite indexes. Real-time listeners via onSnapshot stream document and query changes instantly to connected clients. Security rules in firestore.rules enforce read/write access per-path using request.auth, resource.data, and custom functions. Transactions with runTransaction guarantee atomic read-then-write consistency, while writeBatch groups up to 500 writes into a single atomic commit. Offline persistence is enabled by default on mobile and can be enabled on web with enableIndexedDbPersistence, caching reads and queuing writes until connectivity resumes. Server-side access via the Firebase Admin SDK bypasses security rules for backend operations, Cloud Functions triggers, and data migrations.

sections.title: Document/collection data model
sections.body: Firestore stores data in documents organized within collections. Each document is identified by a unique ID within its collection and contains key-value fields. Documents can hold subcollections, enabling hierarchical data modeling. Supported field types: string, number, boolean, timestamp, array, map, reference, geopoint, and null. Maximum document size is 1 MB. Collection names and document IDs are case-sensitive.

sections.title: CRUD operations
sections.body: Create documents with addDoc (auto-ID) or setDoc (explicit ID). Read with getDoc (single) or getDocs (query results). Update fields with updateDoc, supporting nested dot-notation paths, arrayUnion/arrayRemove, increment, serverTimestamp, and deleteField. Delete with deleteDoc. setDoc with { merge: true } performs a partial update like updateDoc but creates the document if it does not exist.

sections.title: Queries and filtering
sections.body: Build queries with query() chaining where(), orderBy(), limit(), startAt(), startAfter(), endAt(), endBefore(). Where operators: ==, !=, <, <=, >, >=, in, not-in, array-contains, array-contains-any. Compound queries combining equality on one field with range/orderBy on another require composite indexes. getDocs() executes a query once; onSnapshot() streams real-time updates.

sections.title: Real-time listeners
sections.body: onSnapshot attaches a listener to a document reference or query that fires immediately with current data, then again on every change. Snapshot includes docChanges() to distinguish 'added', 'modified', and 'removed' events. The returned unsubscribe function stops the listener. Listeners work offline and automatically re-sync when connectivity resumes. Use snapshotEqual() to compare snapshots.

sections.title: Security rules
sections.body: Firestore Security Rules are defined in firestore.rules and enforce access control at the document path level. Rules use request.auth for the authenticated user, request.resource.data for incoming writes, and resource.data for the existing document. Custom functions factor out shared logic. Rules support get() and exists() to read other documents during evaluation. Deploy rules with 'firebase deploy --only firestore:rules'.

sections.title: Transactions and batched writes
sections.body: runTransaction executes a function that can read and write documents atomically. If a read document is modified by another client during the transaction, Firestore retries automatically (up to 5 times). writeBatch groups up to 500 set/update/delete operations into a single atomic commit without reading. Both guarantee all-or-nothing semantics. Transactions run client-side; for server-side transactions use the Admin SDK.

sections.title: Offline persistence
sections.body: Offline persistence caches Firestore data locally, serving reads from cache and queuing writes until connectivity resumes. Enabled by default on iOS and Android. On web, call enableIndexedDbPersistence() — only one tab can use persistence at a time. Use enableMultiTabIndexedDbPersistence() for multi-tab support. Cached data persists across app restarts. Pending writes are retried automatically in order.
